title: Understanding Open Fontanel (Molera) in Dogs | Veterinary Triage Guide
meta_desc: Learn about open fontanels in dogs, why they occur, and when to seek veterinary care for your pet's soft spot. Urgency level: Low.
content_html:
What is an Open Fontanel?

An open fontanel, also known as a 'molera,' is a small gap or soft spot on the top of a dog's skull where the cranial bones have not fully fused. This condition is most common in toy breeds, particularly Chihuahuas, and is often present from birth. While it sounds frightening, it is a known characteristic in several small breeds.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for an open fontanel is Low. If your dog is behaving normally, meeting developmental milestones, and showing no signs of neurological distress, it is not an emergency. However, because the brain is less protected in that specific area, you should be extra cautious about head trauma and mention it to your veterinarian during your next routine appointment.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Sharing a photo or a brief video of the top of your dog's head helps veterinary professionals assess the size and location of the opening. Visuals allow us to check for any bulging, pulsing, or skin irritation over the site, which helps us determine if the fontanel is a benign physical trait or a symptom of an underlying condition like hydrocephalus.

faqs.question: Will my puppy's soft spot eventually close?
faqs.answer: In many puppies, the fontanel will naturally close or significantly decrease in size by 3 to 6 months of age. In some small breeds, a tiny opening may remain for the duration of the dog's life.

faqs.question: Are there specific symptoms I should watch for?
faqs.answer: Yes. While the spot itself isn't usually an issue, you should seek veterinary care if you notice seizures, a dome-shaped head, downward-slanting eyes, or lack of coordination.

faqs.question: How should I handle a dog with an open fontanel?
faqs.answer: Be gentle when petting the top of the head and avoid rough play with larger animals. It is also recommended to use a harness rather than a collar to avoid putting any unnecessary pressure on the neck and head area.
